summary: Joel Spolsky argues that computer science programs have increasingly shifted to teaching Java exclusively, abandoning harder concepts like pointers (C/C++) and recursion (functional programming). He contends that these difficult topics served as important filters that separated strong programmers from weaker ones. The post is a self-aware lament that universities are dumbing down curricula to reduce dropout rates, producing graduates who lack the ability to work on truly challenging systems-level problems. Spolsky acknowledges the 'get off my lawn' quality of the argument but maintains it reflects a real decline in CS education rigor.
key_points: Java-only CS curricula eliminate the two most important conceptual weeding-out topics: pointers and recursion
Universities switched to Java not because it produces better programmers, but because it reduces dropout rates and complaints
The ability to understand pointers and recursion is a fundamental indicator of whether someone can think about complex abstractions
This trend produces graduates who can build routine business software but struggle with genuinely hard problems like distributed systems or compiler design
money_quotes: A sure sign of my descent into senility is bitchin' and moanin' about 'kids these days,' and how they won't or can't do anything hard any more.
key_insight: By removing pointers and recursion from CS curricula in favor of Java-only education, universities eliminated the very concepts that distinguish strong systems programmers from mediocre ones.
